sentences.text: The tunnel in Dora was-- had traffic going through it.
It was like a little city, actually.
The other [? parts ?] that were on the opposite end on the early side, on the Harzungen side, they was still digging on that end.
But it was so deep underground that the type of bombs during the Second World War could not penetrate that tunnel.
That's how deep it was underground.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We-- life in camp goes on in a day-to-day basis, as I said earlier.
In January, I would say, I know it was in the winter of 1945, a group of us were taken to unload a train.
We don't know where-- what we're going to unload, but we're being marched to the railroad station.
That train brought in a load of people that they-- they evacuated Auschwitz.
And these prisoners, we found out when we unloaded them, that they-- they're coming from Auschwitz.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, we're-- we were supposed to unload that train.
When they tell these prisoners to disembark, there were many dead bodies left in the-- in the wagons.
Our job was to clean the wagons, take the bodies out.
They picked a spot next to the railroad station there.
And we used to bring the bodies.
They-- they put layers of bodies and wood.
There was a layer of bodies, a layer of wood.
When the pile was completed, they put gasoline on it, and they lit it.
And they-- they got-- they burned the bodies that way.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We asked these people that we can talk to, which they looked a lot worse than even we did, we asked them what was going on.
In dribs and drabs, we're starting to hear that the war is going bad for Germany.
We heard rumors, but we-- this time we heard it from prisoners that were evacuated from Auschwitz because the Russian front got close to Auschwitz.
And Germany, the-- the SS did not want any of the prisoners to be liberated by the Allies.
So we heard little rumors here and there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After that work detail, these prisoners blended in with the rest of the camp.
The rest-- the rest of the camp went about going to work every day and standing appell in the morning and at night.
Things were going as-- as routinely as possible at the camp.
Camp life went on.
How were you working underground?
For-- for the B-1 missiles?
It was not too long because after having these-- all these various jobs, it wasn't too long before Dora was evacuated.
I would say in February of '45.
We're all standing, what they called Zahlappell, where everybody's standing out and we're being counted.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We're all given, each one of us, in the past we each got a loaf of bread the size of a brick for three people, each one of us is given a loaf of bread per person.
We're being told we're being-- we're going to another camp.
This piece of bread is enough for three days for you, and after that, you'll be fed again.
Some people, being that we were so hungry, finished that-- that loaf of bread within an hour or two.
And some people tried to ration the bread and listen to what they told us.
That it will have to last us for three days.
I tried to ration it.
I would not eat it right away.

We're march-- they're marching us, which was a familiar sight to us by now, to the same wagons that we came to Auschwitz that we just not too long ago unloaded, the Auschwitz evacuees that came into Dora.
We got into-- into this-- into this cattle train-- train cars.

Some cars-- they didn't have enough cars, some cars were the closed type that we used to come in, and some cars were the open type of cars that is used for-- to carry different merchandise other than humans.
The people that were on the inside in the closed cars, which I was lucky enough to be in one, were so-called the lucky ones.
The people that work in the open cars were-- were loaded to the point not-- you could not sit.
You had to stand and start from the end of the car and stand, one body against the other, until the car filled up.
Everybody had to stand up.
The train is going now a day, two, three.

As the train is-- as the train is moving, especially in the open trains, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people were so crowded and-- and so uncomfortable that a lot of prisoners, and there was a mixture, you didn't know-- not that you didn't know, you-- there were Jewish prisoners, Polish, Ukrainians, Germans.
You could see by-- by-- by their emblems.
Some of the prisoners, in order to get a little more comfortable, it was a matter of the stronger surviving.
Again, it was a survival of the fittest.
They would pick the weaker, throw them overboard as the train was going to wherever we went to, so they should have a little more room.
We got to a point, I don't know where we were at, rumors were that they bombed the railroad ahead of us.
They let us all get off the train.
The guards surrounded the train, and we were just sitting and waiting for-- to see what else they're going to tell us to do.
At this point, I was just with some of the kids, some of the people that I befriended in the camps and some of the people that I helped when I worked in the kitchen that I used to bring them food.
We sort of tried to stay together.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were, for-- for the better part of a day, loose where we can stretch our legs, and we were surrounded, the train was surrounded, and we were able to move about.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As I-- at that point, my bread was long gone.
We didn't get fed anymore, and we were just hungry.
As hungry can be.
I-- I spotted a discarded tube of toothpaste, and I picked it up.
I really didn't know why I picked it up.
Maybe I was curious to see what it was.
And I put it in my pocket.
We were given orders to get back onto the train, and we are continuing our journey.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We don't have any food.
We don't have any water.
My mouth is dry.
My-- my tongue is-- I don't know how, I got-- I found my-- my-- that discarded, it was a discarded tube, but it still had a little bit of toothpaste in it.
And I squeezed a little bit of toothpaste-- toothpaste on my tip of my finger and, if I licked it, it sort of created saliva in my mouth.
The-- the taste of the toothpaste took away the dryness.

It sort of created-- That-- that toothpaste, really that-- that empty tube of toothpaste, until I was able to-- in fact, I ripped open the-- the tube so I can get in more as much as possible of that toothpaste until-- till we got to where we were going.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The train stops and the usual [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] orders were barked at us, [SPEAKING GERMAN], which we were used to already.
We got out of the-- got off the trains.
There were many-- many of us that didn't make it that were left in the trains dead.
The ones that were able to walk, they walked us, it wasn't a far walk, maybe an hour.
It wasn't a far walk.
I can't remember how far it was, but not too far.
From sitting so long, we were all sort of stiff, and we couldn't walk and besides, a lot from hunger and not having any water.
We were walking from the train into the camp.
We find out the name of the camp is called Bergen-Belsen.
As we were walking from the trains into the camp, anybody that could not walk was shot.
There was no questions asked.
Was shot.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The will to live was so strong, this guy that was walking besides me, couldn't walk.
He fell down.
He got shot in the head.
I saw the blood gushing out of his head.
His will to live was so strong he tried to get back on his feet to continue walking, but he fell down.
He was dead.
He couldn't-- couldn't continue.
I was kind-- my legs were kind of stiff, and I needed a little assistance to walk myself.
I was barely 14 at the time.
I should have been able to-- to jump, but I couldn't even walk.

Some of my friends that I used to help with food, when I used to sort of bring the food into the camp from-- from the kitchen that I used to work in, they were walking alongside me, took one of my arms and put it on-- on each of their shoulders, and I would-- I walked into camp that way.
We came into the camp.
We were all assigned to barracks where we slept.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We did not work in that camp.
We were warehoused, for a better word.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Epidemic broke out.
Typhus.
Cholera.
Where people had very-- they were burning up from the temperature, the high temperature, and they would-- people would drop.
It was very common to see dead bodies all over, all around you.
We were sleeping in those carts, like-- like those shelves, like 10-- 10 to a cart, like-- They used to come in once a day with a watered down, what we called kolorabi soup.
A lot of people were very weak.
They couldn't get down to-- to get their soup.
So they would go around-- fellow prisoners would go around with a-- with a can, it looked like what we know today as like a garbage can, with the soup was in it with a ladle.
We had our plates.
And if you just held your hand out, as weak as you were, they would pour a ladle of soup into the-- into your plate.
And that continued.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Naturally I was hungry.
That-- that soup was not enough.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know who the-- the man was next to me.
The man died.
Normally when a-- when-- when somebody died, they used to pick up the bodies or the prisoners that the body died next to, were thrown down to the floor, and they would pick up the bodies.
I didn't throw that body down.
Every time they came for the soup, I would take his arm, and I would sort of fling it.
I flung it-- where the-- where the-- They didn't check because people that were alive looked more like they were dead.
So I would have a little bit of extra soup from this.
Because of this body.
I don't know who he was.
I never-- I don't know his name.
After a while, I couldn't keep him anymore because rigor mortis set in.
I just couldn't keep him there any more.
But it didn't take long with another prisoner.
Same thing happened.
I did this with two prisoners.
I don't know who they were.
I never knew their names.
They-- the ones-- we still had to go out and still stand in that Zahlappell, what they call.
Whether you were weak, no matter what, everybody had to go.
No matter how sick you were, you had to go and stand out on appell.
We started to see the guards, the SS, with white armbands walking around.
Rumor started to say that this meant something that is good in our favor.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] One day, it's April 15, 1945.
We're standing out on appell.
As we were standing out in appell, a-- two Jeeps of soldiers drive up.
We still look.
We're still stunned.
We're still afraid to move.
We see the Germans go over to them, and they hand them their guns.
There were British soldiers.
We did not know the difference in uniform.
We knew that-- we knew they were not German uniforms, but we didn't know-- we did not know they were German, or they were British.
As soon as it set in, in our minds, what is happening, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I saw the first lynching in my life.
A kapo, who was a real, real bad man, who didn't treat his fellow prisoners right, was the first man that I've seen-- first of a few, that I've seen being lynched.
They actually beat him to death.
Nobody stopped anybody.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It happened to a couple of-- few people like this that I've seen happen.
Yes.
We were all-- we were all standing outside.
We were all-- we were just liberated, but we didn't comprehend what was happening at-- to us.
But as soon as we realized what was happening, the other prisoners took it out on the-- on the-- on the prisoners that were-- that were-- that didn't behave to us the way they should have.
Now we're liberated, but for some reason, the British army didn't let us-- we were still in the camp.
We still cannot get out of the camp.
The army is continuing because the war is not over yet.
They are continuing on.
Just a few soldiers are left with the camp.
And the-- Hungary was an ally of Germany.
There was a Hungarian military detachment close by, apparently.
To this day, I don't understand why.
These Hungarians, they were, in many cases, worse to us than the Germans when we were in the-- we were in the ghetto and before.
They were put in charge to watch us.
We were liberated already.
We could not get out of the camp.
A day or two later, they were withdrawn.
We're going-- we're-- we're moving around.
We know we can get out of the camp.
I'll not-- I'll never forget.
When I got out of the gate, I was first of all-- I was afraid to-- I knew I was liberated.
I was afraid to walk out that gate.
And every time I walk-- when I walk out that gate, I kept looking back if somebody is after me to take me back in.
We see English bulldozers making big, big graves.
We see captured our former guards, captured SS, loading dead bodies.
The bodies were stacked up like a hill, high up, and they were loading them on trucks.
And the trucks were just like a dump truck.
The trucks would just dump them into the-- into the mass big grave that the bulldozers were-- were digging and the bodies fell down with the hands, legs dangling.
It would be like-- you didn't-- we were so hardened and watched this like you were watching mannequins being buried.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We also had former soldiers in the camps with us.
Russian prisoners of war.
They-- they knew how to handle weapons and how to-- and they took eventually some of the SS.
They were-- they captured some of these SS and they were-- they killed them.
Beat them to death.
And after a while, these things subsided.
They made all kinds of pictures.
I remember the British, there must have been a news photographer, news reel people.
They took pictures of us, but I don't know whatever happened to these pictures.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After a while, some of us that were a little stronger than others, when we got out of the camp, they went into the German houses in the surrounding area.
And they were-- they were taking whatever food they could find in the-- in the-- and they-- they had-- they didn't have a problem of-- with food.
They-- there was no law and order at the time.
They could have robbed.
They could have taken gold, diamonds, any kind of valuables and nobody would have done anything to them.
The only thing they were looking to take was bread and anything that was able-- we were able to eat.
And they would bring it back to the ones that didn't go out to those houses to accumulate all that food.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After a while, things got a little better.

The-- we got-- the problem was that we were so-- our stomachs and our insides were so dried up from lack of food, that-- I would say thousands of people died after they were liberated from overeating.
Because they ate anything they could put-- lay their hands on.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Pieces of bacon, ham, whatever they-- It was fat.
Fat-- food that was too rich for the intestines at that time because we weren't used to-- our bellies weren't used to have food in them.
So people would drop after they were liberated.
A lot of people.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well then, life is-- they-- we're being fed by the, I assume, British army.
Slowly we were trying to-- we are still in prisoner clothes.
Some of us that found regular civilian type of a coat would put it on, to-- to bring him back to the civilian life.
To remember how we used to be dressed or whatever.
A month-- a month or two after the liberation, we were still in Bergen-Belsen.
I was in the hospital at the time, simply because of being weak from malnutrition or whatever.
There is a rumor going around that the Swedish Red Cross is-- they're taking 10,000 former prisoners from the concentration camps to recuperate and to bring them back to a normal life.
They're taking them to Sweden.
The only criteria was if somebody was sick, or weak, and everybody fell in that category.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I wanted to get away from Germany as fast as I could.
Because I was in the hospital at the time, I was one of the guys that was being taken to Sweden.
The war is over for us, and we are being taken to Sweden.
What happened was, when I was liberated and I knew already what happened to our families, at that point I-- I believed what happened to our families, I didn't want to go back to my hometown anymore.
Although my father survived, he did go back to our hometown, but I did not know it.
Some of us that went to Sweden, went to Sweden.
Some of us went back to our hometown or to the surrounding towns that I come from.
The man that I used to help, that I befriended, that I made acquaintance with in-- in the ghetto, that I use to help him with food when I worked in the kitchen, he was liberated with me.
And he went back to his hometown, which was in the same county that I come from.
In order to get to his home town, he had to pass my town.
In those days, it was very shortly after the war, there was no-- no transportation.
So people either walked, traveled by horse and buggy, or any way they could.
He passed my town.
Out of the 83 families that lived in my town, there were two Jewish men in my town.
My father and one other man by the name of [?
Schmiel ?] [?
Stover. ?] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] As he passed the town, my father remembered this man from-- from the ghetto, naturally the thing to ask was, did anybody survive?
Did you see my son?
Did you see my daughter?
Did you see my wife?
Do you know if anybody survived?
This man told my father, yes, your-- your son is alive.
He was liberated.
I left him in Bergen-Belsen.
He did not know at the time that I wound up going to Sweden.
Now my father knows I'm alive.
He knows I'm somewheres.
He doesn't know where.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
We-- we came to Sweden.
Most of us still came in our prisoner's striped clothes.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were all full of lice.
We-- a lot of us were sick.
And had-- a lot of us had impetigo, that itchy type of rash.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They-- I have to say and thank the Swedish people from the bottom of my heart.
They were just a sight that we could use at that time.
They were absolutely wonderful to us.
We weren't just Jewish prisoners.
We were from all countries, all walk of life, all kinds of ages, whoever survived.
They made us undress.
Took off all our dirty clothes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They put-- oh yeah.
First they put us in a quarantine.
OK.
